title: Chapter IV.

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: “I have often noticed this, Sulpitius, that Martin was accustomed to say to you, that such an abundance of power was by no means granted him while he was a bishop, as he remembered to have possessed before he obtained that office.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Now, if this be true, or rather since it is true, we may imagine how great those things were which, while still a monk, he accomplished, and which, without any witness, he effected apart by himself; since we have seen that, while a bishop, he performed so great wonders before the eyes of all.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Many, no doubt, of his former achievements were known to the world, and could not be hid, but those are said to have been innumerable which, while he avoided boastfulness, he kept concealed and did not allow to come to the knowledge of mankind; for, inasmuch as he transcended the capabilities of mere man, in a consciousness of his own eminence, and trampling upon worldly glory, he was content simply to have heaven as a witness of his deeds.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: That this is true we can judge even from these things which are well known to us, and could not be hid; since e.g. before he became a bishop he restored two dead men to life, facts of which your book has treated pretty fully, but, while he was bishop, he raised up only one, a point which I am surprised you have not noticed.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: I myself am a witness to this latter occurrence; but, probably, you have no doubts about the matter being duly testified.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: At any rate, I will set before you the affair as it happened.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: For some reason, I know not what, we were on our way to the town of the Carnutes.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: In the meantime, as we pass by a certain village most populous in inhabitants, an enormous crowd went forth to meet us, consisting entirely of heathen; for no one in that village was acquainted with a Christian.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Nevertheless, owing to the report of the approach of so great a man, a multitude of those streaming to one point had filled all the widely spreading plains.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Martin felt that some work was to be performed; and as the spirit within him was thus moving him, he was deeply excited.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: He at once began to preach to the heathen the word of God, so utterly different from that of man, often groaning that so great a crowd should be ignorant of the Lord the Saviour.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: In the meantime, while an incredible multitude had surrounded us, a certain woman, whose son had recently died, began to present, with outstretched hands, the lifeless body to the blessed man, saying, “We know that you are a friend of God: restore me my son, who is my only one.”

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: The rest of the multitude joined her, and added their entreaties to those of the mother.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Martin perceiving, as he afterwards told us, that he could manifest power, in order to the salvation of those waiting for its display, received the body of the deceased into his own hands; and when, in the sight of all, he had fallen on his knees, and then arose, after his prayer was finished, he restored to its mother the child brought back to life.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Then, truly, the whole multitude, raising a shout to heaven, acknowledged Christ as God, and finally began to rush in crowds to the knees of the blessed man, sincerely imploring that he would make them Christians.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Nor did he delay to do so.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: As they were in the middle of the plain, he made them all catechumens, by placing his hand upon the whole of them; while, at the same time, turning to us, he said that, not without reason, were these made catechumens in that plain where the martyrs were wont to be consecrated.”
